participants.extracted: Adults aged 18 years and older who smoked at least 5 cigarettes per day and lived in low socioeconomic status areas of a Midwestern city. The randomized comparison included 135 participants assigned to mindfulness training for smokers (MTS, n=67) or enhanced Freedom From Smoking (FFS-E, n=68), with an additional 40 participants choosing the lower-intensity quit line option. Overall, the sample had a mean age of 44.5 years, 46.7% were female, and 88.1% were Caucasian.

interventions.extracted: Participants who chose the higher-intensity smoking cessation intervention were randomized to either Mindfulness Training for Smokers (MTS) or an enhanced Freedom from Smoking program (FFS-E). MTS consisted of seven weekly 2.5-hour classes plus a 6.5-hour Quit Day Retreat (24 total hours), with instruction in mindful meditation, walking, smoking, eating, and mindful management of triggers, urges, addictive thoughts, and emotions; classes included guided practice, group support, and daily home meditation practice of 15–30 minutes using a CD. FFS-E was designed to closely match MTS in time, intensity, pharmacotherapy, instructor qualifications, materials, and home practice, but replaced mindfulness with cognitive-behavioral smoking cessation content and emphasized relaxation practice, including 15–30 minutes of relaxation in each class, daily guided relaxation at home, logged practice time, and weekly discussion. Both interventions provided two weeks of nicotine patches and an optional long-term weekly support group.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure smoking abstinence as the primary outcome using 7-day point-prevalence abstinence at 4 weeks and 24 weeks post-quit day, confirmed by carbon monoxide breath testing and the Timeline Follow-Back smoking calendar. Additional smoking outcomes included number of days smoked in the first 2 weeks post-quit and self-reported urge intensity assessed by three phone calls before the intervention and three phone calls after the quit day using a 0–10 rating scale. Secondary psychological outcomes included mindfulness measured with the Five-Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ) at baseline, 4 weeks, and 24 weeks post-quit; experiential avoidance measured with the Acceptance and Action Questionnaire (AAQ) at baseline, 4 weeks, and 24 weeks; and perceived stress measured with the Perceived Stress Scale-10 (PSS-10) at baseline, 4 weeks, and 24 weeks. The study also assessed compliance with daily meditation or relaxation practice using a Meditation/Relaxation Calendar from Class 1 through the 4-week assessment visit, and class attendance/attrition during the intervention period.
